generated_note:
**Subjective**
Chief Complaint: Feels pretty good overall but has noticed he's been a little more tired than usual, which he attributed to getting older.
Social History: Smoked in college for about two years, roughly a pack a week, quit at age twenty-three, hasn't smoked since. Alcohol is about four to six drinks a week — a couple beers on the weekend, glass of wine with dinner once or twice a week. Denies recreational drug use currently, tried marijuana a couple times in college. Exercise has declined — he used to play basketball twice a week but that fell apart, now he's walking the dog about twenty minutes most mornings and getting on the Peloton maybe once a week. So roughly two to three days a week of moderate activity. Diet — skips breakfast, just coffee with cream and sugar in the morning. Lunch is usually from the work cafeteria, tries for salads but often ends up with sandwiches. Dinner is home-cooked by his wife, generally healthy — chicken, vegetables, rice. His main issue is nighttime snacking, chips and crackers. Sleep is about six to six and a half hours a night, up late working or watching TV, alarm at six.
Family History: Father is sixty-nine, had a myocardial infarction at sixty-two, currently on medications. Mother is sixty-seven, has type 2 diabetes diagnosed about ten years ago and hypertension. One brother, forty-two, healthy. One sister, forty-eight, has hypercholesterolemia. Maternal grandmother also had type 2 diabetes. Maternal grandfather had colon cancer in his seventies. Paternal aunt had breast cancer.
Review of Systems: Occasional tension headaches, managed with ibuprofen. Got reading glasses last year, eye doctor said age-appropriate. No hearing problems. No chest pain. Does note he gets a little more winded going up stairs than he used to. No cough or wheezing. Heartburn once or twice a month, usually after spicy food. No abdominal pain. Bowel habits normal. Nocturia occasionally, once a night, not every night. Right knee stiffness after Peloton use. No concerning skin changes noted by the patient. Mood is generally stable, gets work-related stress but denies depression. No sexual dysfunction.

Not documented

Not documented

No known drug allergies

Tobacco: Smoked in college for about two years, roughly a pack a week, quit at age twenty-three, hasn't smoked since.

**Objective**
**Physical Examination**
Vital Signs:
BP: One-thirty-two over eighty-four
HR: Seventy-six
Temp: Ninety-eight point four
RR: Fourteen
SpO2: Ninety-eight percent on room air
Height: Five-ten
Weight: Two-oh-six pounds
BMI: Twenty-nine point six

General:
(Only document examination findings that were explicitly mentioned. If no physical exam was performed, state "Physical examination not documented")
Head and neck: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light, conjunctivae clear. Tympanic membranes normal bilaterally. Nares clear. Oropharynx without lesions, tonsils normal. Thyroid normal size, no nodules palpated. No lymphadenopathy in the neck, axillae, or groin.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, gallops, or rubs.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally with good air movement.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly, normal bowel sounds, no masses.
Skin: Some sun damage noted on the forearms and back of the neck, nothing that looks atypical or worrisome, no concerning moles identified.
Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion in all extremities. Right knee with no swelling, no instability, good range of motion.
Neurological: Alert and oriented, cranial nerves intact, sensation and strength normal.
Genitourinary: Testes normal, no masses, no hernias.

**Assessment**
Borderline stage one hypertension at one-thirty-two over eighty-four, up from one-twenty-six over seventy-eight last year.
Pre-diabetes — fasting glucose one-oh-four, A1C five point seven.
Dyslipidemia — elevated LDL at one-forty-one, low HDL at forty-four, elevated triglycerides at one-eighty-five.
Overweight — BMI twenty-nine point six.
Fatigue — most likely related to chronic sleep deprivation, averaging six to six and a half hours per night.
Right knee stiffness with exercise — no swelling or instability on exam, likely mechanical.
Occasional heartburn — once or twice a month with spicy food.

**Plan**
1. Lifestyle modification — exercise, weight loss, dietary sodium reduction — and recheck blood pressure in one month. Will reassess need for pharmacotherapy at that time.
2. Counseled on lifestyle modifications including increased physical activity, dietary changes particularly reducing refined carbohydrates and nighttime snacking, and weight loss. Goal weight loss of fifteen to twenty pounds discussed. Recheck fasting glucose and lipids in three months.
3. Aggressive lifestyle modification first. If LDL does not improve in three to six months, will discuss initiating a statin.
4. Addressed as part of overall cardiovascular and metabolic risk reduction.
5. Counseled that he needs a minimum of seven hours and that sleep affects weight, blood sugar, blood pressure, and energy levels.
6. Advised to monitor.
7. Gave Td booster today, as his last Tdap was in two thousand fifteen. Administered the flu vaccine today. COVID booster was done last October, up to date. Hepatitis B series confirmed in records. Shingles and pneumococcal vaccines deferred, not yet indicated at his age.
8. Placed referral for colonoscopy, given he's forty-five and has a family history of colon cancer in his maternal grandfather. Also placing a referral to dermatology for a full skin check given sun damage noted on exam and history of significant sun exposure in childhood.
9. Counseled on warning signs of myocardial infarction and stroke — chest pain, pressure, shortness of breath, arm or jaw pain, sudden weakness, speech changes, facial drooping — and instructed him to go to the ER immediately if any of those occur.
10. Was pretty direct with him about the fact that he's at a fork in the road here and that lifestyle changes are not optional. He was receptive and said he'd discuss it with his wife tonight. He'll follow up with me in three months for reassessment of blood pressure, glucose, and lipids, and to review any referral results.
